title: Make ten lights flow like water
learnerProfile: adult beginner; curious and capable; no electronics assumed
mission: Wire a ten-segment LED bar — each segment on its own pin through its own 220 Ω resistor — and drive all ten from a sketch that never names a pin more than once. Ten pins live in one array; one loop walks that array and lights whichever pin it points at. The sweep you see is the loop's counter climbing through the list and back. Learn that, and adding an eleventh output later is one more number, not another block of copied code.

sourceMetadata.imageAlt: Official Freenove circuit for the LED Bar: ten LED segments, each driven from its own GPIO pin through a 220 ohm resistor and returning through a shared common row to ground, shown as both a schematic and a breadboard photo.
sourceMetadata.licenseNote: Based on Freenove official material released under CC BY-NC-SA 3.0; preserve attribution and non-affiliation language.

parts.name: ESP32-S3 board
parts.explanation: A development board is a friendly package around a tiny computer chip. The ESP32-S3 runs your uploaded sketch, controls pins, reads sensors, and can also use USB, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth. In this lesson, it sends the ping signal and measures the echo time.

parts.name: GPIO extension board
parts.explanation: GPIO means general-purpose input/output. These pins are connection points your code can use. The extension board makes them easier to see, reach, and wire without cramming everything onto the ESP32-S3 itself.

parts.name: LED bar graph
parts.explanation: A LED bar packs ten separate LEDs into one strip, with a row of pins along each edge. Each LED is wired and driven on its own — exactly like the single LED from earlier days, just ten of them in a tidy line.

parts.name: 10 × 220 Ω resistors
parts.explanation: A resistor limits how much current flows. Placed in series with the LED, the 220 Ω one keeps the current gentle enough to light the LED instead of burning it out. The bands of colour print its value, and it has no direction.

parts.name: Jumper wires
parts.explanation: Jumper wires make temporary connections without soldering. Female-to-male wires have a socket on one end and a pin on the other, useful when a sensor module and board use different connector styles.

parts.name: Arduino IDE
parts.explanation: Arduino IDE is the desktop app that opens the sketch, compiles it, uploads it to the ESP32-S3, and shows messages from the board in Serial Monitor.

wiring.reason: Each segment gets its own pin so code can address them one at a time. This exact order is the order the array uses. Its 220 Ω resistor sets the current that keeps that segment alive.
Every segment returns through one shared row to zero volts

coachInstructions: Guide one segment at a time and wait for learner confirmation before moving to the next; ten connections are easy to lose track of.

Teach the core idea once the circuit works- ten pins in one array, one loop stepping through it by index, so code written once drives all ten. Frame the moving light as one counter climbing through the list over time.

Explain the resistor as current-limiting, not decoration- a LED alone pulls too much current and burns out, and the 220 Ω resistor in series sets the current it runs on. Each segment needs its own; a shared or missing resistor is the common mistake here.
If the whole bar stays dark, have the learner rotate the LED bar 180° and re-check the common row to ground before changing code.
If one segment never lights, check that segment's resistor and its jumper to its GPIO pin, not the whole circuit — its neighbours working proves the code and ground are fine.
For the challenge, have them edit only the array (reorder, then shorten) and predict the result first; the point is that the pattern lives in the list, not the loop or the wiring.
Keep the Arduino path primary; MicroPython is optional only if already set up.
steps: Seat the ESP32-S3 on the GPIO extension board and keep USB unplugged while you wire.
Place the LED bar across the breadboard's centre channel so each segment has its own row.
Wire each segment through its own 220 Ω resistor to its GPIO pin, working along the list in order.
Wire the bar's common row to ground so every segment shares the same return.
Compare every wire to the chart before you plug in USB.
Open Sketch_03.1_FlowingLight.ino in Arduino IDE and upload it.
Watch a single lit segment sweep along the bar and back.

arduino.explanation: All ten pins in one array, in bar order. The array holds the pattern; position 0 is the first segment, position 9 the last.
Counts the array so the loop knows where to stop. Add or remove a pin and the loops adjust on their own, with no other edit.
Runs once per pin in setup(), so a single line prepares all ten instead of ten copied lines.
ledPins[i] reads the pin at position i, and the loop drives just that one HIGH; the next line sets it LOW before i moves on.
Same array, index counting down from 9 to 0, so the light steps back the other way.

micropython.explanation: The same list of pins in Python, in the same bar order.
Picks one pin from the list and drives it, then the loop moves to the next.

theoryModel.plainLanguage: Ten pins live in one array; a loop's counter steps through it, lighting whichever pin its index points at. The moving light is that index over time.
theoryModel.formula: pins in an array + a loop over the index = ten outputs from code you wrote once

notes.title: Why an array beats ten variables
notes.body: Ten named variables would mean ten copies of pinMode and two more of every blink line. The array collapses all of that into one loop, and it takes the count of pins from the list itself — so growing the bar is one more number, not another block of code.

notes.title: Why each segment still needs its own resistor
notes.body: A LED left to itself pulls far more current than it can survive and burns out in an instant. It has no way to limit itself. The 220 Ω resistor in series does the limiting — the voltage across it sets how much current flows, and that current is what a LED actually runs on. Ten LEDs on ten pins means ten resistors; one shared resistor would starve or overload them together.

expectedOutputExample: One lit segment starts at one end of the bar.
It travels along the bar a segment at a time to the far end.
Then it flows back the other way, and repeats for as long as the board has power.
test.successCriteria: Only one segment is lit at any instant, so the flow you see is that single light stepping from one row to the next.

firstChecks: The bar is likely in backwards — its direction is easy to reverse. Rotate it 180°, then re-check the common row to ground.
That one segment is a broken circuit of its own — check its 220 Ω resistor and its jumper to the right GPIO pin. Its neighbours working tells you the code and ground are fine.

troubleshooting.symptom: Segments light in a scrambled order, not a clean sweep
firstChecks: A jumper is on the wrong GPIO, so a pin sits out of sequence versus the array. Re-check each wire against the pin order 21 47 48 38 39 40 41 42 2 1.
Re-check the common row to ground, then that the sketch actually uploaded.
Swap in a data-capable USB cable.

challenge: Reorder the numbers in the pin array and watch the sweep reorder to match, then delete two numbers and watch the sweep shorten on its own — the loop follows the list without being touched.
